steps.title: Fastest path: mark the circle facts first
steps.content: Mark the circle facts first: radii are equal, a diameter sets up a right angle, a tangent is perpendicular to the radius, and central/inscribed angles connect to arcs.

steps.title: Find the radius and semicircle arc length
steps.content:
From the circumference formula:
$$C = 2\pi r = 96\pi$$
Dividing both sides by $2\pi$: $r = 48$.

Since any semicircle subtends half the circumference, the arc length of a semicircle is:
$$\text{Semicircle arc} = \pi r = 48\pi$$

steps.title: Use the diameter constraints to express arcs in terms of one variable
steps.content:
Since $\overline{AC}$ is a diameter, the arc from $A$ to $C$ (one way) is a semicircle:
$$\text{Arc } AB + \text{Arc } BC = 48\pi \quad (1)$$
Since $\overline{BD}$ is a diameter, the arc from $B$ to $D$ (one way) is a semicircle:
$$\text{Arc } BC + \text{Arc } CD = 48\pi \quad (2)$$
Let Arc $AB = x$. Then Arc $DA = 3x$ (from the problem statement).

From equation (1): $\text{Arc } BC = 48\pi - x$

From equation (2): $\text{Arc } CD = 48\pi - \text{Arc } BC = 48\pi - (48\pi - x) = x$

steps.title: Solve for x using the complete circumference
steps.content:
The four arcs partition the entire circle:
$$\text{Arc } AB + \text{Arc } BC + \text{Arc } CD + \text{Arc } DA = 96\pi$$
Substitute the expressions we found:
$$x + (48\pi - x) + x + 3x = 96\pi$$
$$4x + 48\pi = 96\pi$$
$$4x = 48\pi$$
$$x = 12\pi$$
Therefore:
$$\text{Arc } BC = 48\pi - x = 48\pi - 12\pi = 36\pi$$
D is correct.
